does: This paper audits cross-lingual distributional skew in six frontier large language models by constructing the Cerulean Sea Crisis, a synthetic geopolitical wargame mirroring Eastern Mediterranean territorial disputes. Researchers manipulate only the language of gameplay (English versus Turkish) across 10 games with 5 rounds each, generating 586 validated statements. They employ a zero-shot classifier to measure behavioral dispositions along two continuous dimensions: Concession Rate and Coercive Rhetoric. Results reveal substantial heterogeneous effects: Llama-4 exhibits significantly increased coercive rhetoric in Turkish (+0.800, p=.002), while Gemini-3.1-Pro and DeepSeek-R1 show substantial decreases (-0.750 and -0.860 respectively), demonstrating that language choice alone produces measurable shifts in geopolitical negotiation behavior across different models.
connects: This work extends prior research on multilingual bias and language-dependent behavior in large language models by moving beyond static benchmarks toward dynamic behavioral assessment. It builds on emerging work examining how language choice influences model outputs and decision-making, connecting to broader literatures on distributional bias in NLP systems and cultural alignment in LLMs. The paper responds to concerns about whether frontier models exhibit culturally or linguistically contingent reasoning patterns, and it synthesizes game-theoretic frameworks with adversarial stress-testing methodologies previously applied to study model robustness. The structured wargame design extends simulation-based auditing approaches used to evaluate model reliability under sustained interaction.
enables: This research opens avenues for deeper investigation into what mechanisms cause language-dependent behavioral shifts in LLMs and whether these reflect genuine reasoning differences or artifacts of training data composition and fine-tuning practices. It points toward more sophisticated auditing frameworks that combine controlled experimental design with behavioral assessment, enabling evaluation of how language choice influences high-stakes domains like diplomatic simulation, policy analysis, and conflict modeling. The heterogeneous findings suggest future work should investigate why certain models exhibit opposing effects, potentially illuminating their distinct training regimes. This enables development of diagnostic tools for identifying and potentially mitigating problematic language-dependent biases before deployment in multilingual applications where geopolitical neutrality is critical.
